4 Blood data

In addition to food intake and stress ratings, questionnaire data and blood sampling data were imported to enable manipulation checks and the computation of psychological moderator variables.

4.1 Blood import

[1] "FASTRESS_measures-anthropo-blood(session T1).csv"
[2] "FASTRESS_measures-anthropo-blood(session T2).csv"
[3] "FASTRESS_measures-food(session T1).csv"          
[4] "FASTRESS_measures-food(session T2).csv"          
[5] "FASTRESS_measures-stress-mri(session T1).csv"    
[6] "FASTRESS_measures-stress-mri(session T2).csv"    
[7] "FASTRESS_survey_T1_initial.csv"                  
[8] "FASTRESS_survey_T2_initial.csv"

4.2 Define metabolic condition

Each person × session is assigned a unique condition: -> fasted -> satiated. This is independent of the order of the sessions, as they were randomized.

5 Survey data

5.1 Survey import

# A tibble: 1 × 2
  n_rows n_unique_id
   <int>       <int>
1     40          40
# A tibble: 1 × 2
  n_rows n_unique_id
   <int>       <int>
1     20          20
# A tibble: 0 × 2
# ℹ 2 variables: fastress_id <chr>, n <int>
# A tibble: 0 × 2
# ℹ 2 variables: fastress_id <chr>, n <int>
# A tibble: 1 × 5
  fastress_id    id submitdate          startdate           datestamp          
  <chr>       <dbl> <dttm>              <dttm>              <dttm>             
1 sub-004        35 2026-03-10 11:37:44 2026-03-10 11:24:56 2026-03-10 11:37:44

sub-002 appears twice -> both are T1 -> keep the first one filled out // you did verify this, right? Yes

Keep exactly one row per person and session -> the first one filled out. The second (incorrectly filled out T1) is discarded.

The rule is now: If a questionnaire was filled out multiple times within the same session, the first version filled out was retained.

sub-004 filled out survey T2 twice -> we keep the one from 10.03. as this was her second session.

# A tibble: 0 × 2
# ℹ 2 variables: fastress_id <chr>, n <int>
# A tibble: 0 × 2
# ℹ 2 variables: fastress_id <chr>, n <int>
# A tibble: 0 × 5
# ℹ 5 variables: fastress_id <chr>, id <dbl>, submitdate <dttm>,
#   startdate <dttm>, datestamp <dttm>
# A tibble: 0 × 5
# ℹ 5 variables: fastress_id <chr>, id <dbl>, submitdate <dttm>,
#   startdate <dttm>, datestamp <dttm>

6.2 Sample overview

Result: ids_food is a table with one column (fastress_id) and one row per person containing food data. The same applies to Blood and Survey. This is used to create three “participant lists.”

$food
[1] 40

$blood
[1] 40

$survey
[1] 40

6.3 Dataset overlap

-> Important: Who is in which dataset?

count combination:

# A tibble: 1 × 4
  in_food in_blood in_survey     n
  <lgl>   <lgl>    <lgl>     <int>
1 TRUE    TRUE     TRUE         40

Meaning: How many people are included in each combination?

Examples:
TRUE TRUE TRUE = People with all three data sets
TRUE TRUE FALSE = People with Food + Blood, but without Survey
TRUE FALSE TRUE = Food + Survey, but no Blood

# A tibble: 0 × 1
# ℹ 1 variable: fastress_id <chr>

6.4 Sample Descriptives

Total sample (all with at least one valid session)

Analysis sample (only complete cases for within-subject models)

# A tibble: 3 × 2
  dataset n_persons
  <chr>       <int>
1 food           40
2 blood          40
3 survey         40
# A tibble: 1 × 4
  in_food in_blood in_survey     n

7 Build analysis dataset

7.1 Session merge

7.1.1 Food outcomes

  • total_kcal → total energy intake

  • high_kcal → energy from high-calorie snacks

  • low_kcal → energy from low-calorie snacks

  • prop_high_kcal → proportion of high-calorie energy (shift in preference under stress)

# A tibble: 6 × 2
  item          n
  <chr>     <int>
1 apple        60
2 cake         60
3 chips        60
4 chocolate    60
5 cracker      60
6 nuts         60

  post_gt_pre     n
  <lgl>       <int>
1 FALSE         360
